claim_under_attack: The central uncertainty is not whether cross-tenant learning would help accuracy, but whether compliant tenant-isolated approaches meet minimum product thresholds for enough customers to justify launch.
evidence_or_reason: Side B reframes the core uncertainty without establishing that any minimum performance threshold exists or has been defined. The PRD lists 'improvement over tenant baseline in precision at review capacity' as a success metric, but never specifies what baseline improvement constitutes acceptable fraud detection. If the per-tenant model and anomaly-detection modes cannot be shown to clear a concrete pass/fail threshold — and Side B's own Unknowns section admits the performance gap between compliant and pooled approaches is unknown — then the claim that launch readiness hinges on 'enough customers' meeting thresholds is circular: neither the threshold nor the expected compliant-mode performance is anchored to evidence. The reframe obscures rather than resolves the core decision risk.
